local_names.romanized_local: Naiyou Tang Natie (Chinese), Latte Butterscotch (Malay), Baṭṭarskāṭc Lēṭṭē (Tamil), Baṭaraskôc Laṭṭe (Hindi)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general coffee beverage class; no specific butterscotch latte entry exists. Mapped to closest parent class 'coffee beverage' (FOODON_03301099).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers coffee-based beverages broadly; no dedicated butterscotch latte sub-code exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for butterscotch latte found in the clinical terminology hierarchy; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of a butterscotch latte is one medium café cup of approximately 250 ml (250 g), containing espresso, steamed milk, and butterscotch syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Butterscotch Latte — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Butterscotch Latte (cr_id G00010968), confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — milk-based coffee beverages with added sugar
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for butterscotch latte. GI estimated at 55 by analogy with sweetened milk-based coffee beverages (café latte with sugar) as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and supporting peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care). GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (≤55); classified as low per the standard University of Sydney GI classification scale. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 10.875) / 100 ≈ 6.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar from butterscotch syrup; sodium 35 mg/100 g; potassium 152 mg/100 g below the 200 mg/100 g offset threshold; dietary fibre 0 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sweetened flavoured latte, as the added butterscotch syrup triggers the sweetened flavoured beverage concern gate; an unsweetened plain latte or Americano is a suitable alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar from butterscotch syrup (sugar-sweetened beverage); sugar 4.06 g/100 g with added sugar confirmed during preparation; SSB classification applies regardless of GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage because it contains added butterscotch syrup, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage; a sugar-free syrup substitution or plain unsweetened latte is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar from butterscotch syrup; total fat 1.61 g/100 g (low); cholesterol 7 mg/100 g (low); sodium 35 mg/100 g (moderate); regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this sweetened flavoured latte due to its added sugar content; occasional consumption of a small serving is lower risk than regular daily intake, but an unsweetened latte is preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar from butterscotch syrup with no anti-inflammatory benefit; absence of positive anti-inflammatory factors
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage due to added sugar with no anti-inflammatory value; plain espresso or black coffee, which provides chlorogenic acid, is a preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butterscotch Latte is a sweetened espresso-based coffee beverage made by combining a shot of espresso with steamed milk and butterscotch-flavoured syrup. The drink has a creamy, mildly sweet flavour profile with caramel-like butterscotch notes layered over the bitterness of espresso and the richness of steamed milk. It is commonly served hot in café settings across Singapore and internationally. Per 100 g, it provides 43 kcal, 2.81 g protein, 1.61 g fat, 4.35 g carbohydrate (of which 4.06 g is sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, 35 mg sodium, 152 mg potassium, and 105 mg calcium. The beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to the addition of butterscotch syrup during preparation, which has clinical implications for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The milk component contributes meaningful calcium (105 mg/100 g) and protein (2.81 g/100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low, at the upper boundary of the low GI scale) by analogy with sweetened milk-based coffee beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database. Individuals seeking a lower-sugar alternative should request sugar-free butterscotch syrup or switch to a plain unsweetened latte.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a butterscotch latte?
Is butterscotch latte suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of butterscotch latte?
Is butterscotch latte high in caffeine?
What is the GI of butterscotch latte?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Butterscotch Latte (cr_id G00010968) with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, which reduces GI confidence to medium, but overall record confidence remains high due to the exact HPB source match for nutrient data.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0 g/100 g reported); this is plausible for a milk-and-syrup coffee beverage with no fibre-contributing ingredients. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific butterscotch latte preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16; no approved secondary source provides these values for this exact beverage. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk-based coffee beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for butterscotch latte was found in the approved GI source. GI 55 is classified as low per the standard University of Sydney GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). This beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to confirmed addition of butterscotch syrup during preparation. As this food is commonly consumed in Singapore, multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi have been added to meet G6 requirements. All aliases_by_language entries verified to exist verbatim in the flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3 fix — q4: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 55. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI ≤55 is classified as low; GI 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low category. Updated gi_source notes and natural_language_description to reflect this correction.
